title: Dressed Crab
subtitle: White and brown crab meat seasoned and returned to the shell, a British seafood classic
excerpt: Dressed crab is the process of picking a cooked whole crab, seasoning the brown and white meat separately, and presenting them back in the cleaned shell. There is no cooking involved. The skill is in picking the meat cleanly and seasoning each type correctly: the brown meat boldly, the white meat lightly.
sourceNotes: Traditional British seafood cookery. Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: dressed crab method. Jane Grigson, Fish Cookery (1973): crab preparation. British coastal and fishmonger tradition.

content.text: Buy cooked whole crabs from a fishmonger where you can. A crab that has been cooked and refrigerated the same day will have sweeter, firmer meat than one that has been sitting on a supermarket shelf. A 700 g to 800 g crab produces enough
for one generous or two modest portions.
The intermediate rating is for the
: picking a whole crab takes time and patience, and finding all the meat in the body cavities requires practice. If you buy pre-picked crab meat, the recipe becomes straightforward.

items.name: Crab meat
items.prepNote: from 2 cooked whole crabs (each approx. 700-800 g), or 300g picked white meat and 100g brown meat

items.name: Cayenne pepper
items.prepNote: a pinch only; use half or less to taste

content.text: Keep the white and brown crab meat separate throughout. Season the white meat with the
juice, salt, and a little white pepper. It should be lightly bound, not heavily dressed. Mix the brown meat with the
, and the remaining lemon juice. Add cayenne to taste if using. The brown meat can take bold seasoning; taste and adjust.

If serving in the shell, clean the back shell: remove the carapace, press out the membrane behind the face end to enlarge the cavity, and rinse well. Spoon the brown meat into the centre of the shell. Arrange the white meat on either side. Garnish with a line of chopped parsley between the brown and white meat if using. Serve with brown bread and butter and lemon wedges.

rows.fix: Place the picked white meat in a sieve for 10 minutes before dressing; any excess liquid drains off. Do not add mayonnaise until ready to serve.
rows.cause: Crab was overcooked before purchase, or meat was not drained after picking

rows.fix: Increase the breadcrumbs and add a little more lemon juice. The breadcrumbs absorb the intensity of the brown meat.
rows.cause: This is normal in large crabs; the bread crumbs soften it
rows.symptom: Brown meat is very strong and bitter

rows.fix: Work slowly when cracking claws. Run fingers through the picked white meat feeling for hard fragments before dressing.
rows.cause: Claws not cracked carefully, or body cavities rushed
rows.symptom: Small pieces of shell in the meat

rows.fix: A good 750 g crab should yield about 200 g of meat. If the yield is poor, supplement with bought picked crab meat.
rows.cause: Crabs vary widely in their meat yield; a light crab may have been undersized or undersold
rows.symptom: Not enough meat from the crab

content.text: Dressed crab is a coastal British classic, found in seaside towns from Cornwall to Cromer, which has its own celebrated brown crab tradition. The dish requires a fresh, well-cooked crab and nothing more. It is served cold, at lunch, with brown bread and a wedge of lemon. The technique of dressing it yourself is worth learning once; after that the preparation is straightforward and the result far better than anything from a packet.
